claims.claim: Liang Wenfeng said in a 2024 interview: 'Money has never been the problem for us; bans on shipments of advanced chips are the problem.'

No corroborated 2026 on-the-record statement from Liang Wenfeng personally detailing DeepSeek's Huawei Ascend chip access has been located; disclosure has instead come via DeepSeek's technical reports, Huawei's own statements, and press citing unnamed insiders.
Huawei confirmed its Ascend processors were used in training part of DeepSeek's V4-Flash model; DeepSeek's V4 technical report listed domestic chips alongside Nvidia GPUs for the first time.
DeepSeek's own internal benchmarking, per CSIS, found Huawei's Ascend 910C delivers roughly 60% of an Nvidia H100's inference performance.

At a Feb 17, 2025 symposium, Huawei founder Ren Zhengfei told Xi Jinping he is coordinating a 2,000+-company effort toward 70%+ Chinese semiconductor self-sufficiency by 2028; Liang Wenfeng's attendance is reported only in secondary outlets and he was not among the named speakers.
DeepSeek closed its first-ever external funding round in June 2026, raising ~50 billion yuan (~$7.4B) at a post-money valuation of ~350.88 billion yuan (~$52B), per a Chinese regulatory filing chain.

Commercial investors (Tencent, CATL, JD.com, NetEase, VC funds) invested via a Liang-controlled limited partnership with a five-year lock-up and zero voting rights; China's National AI Industry Investment Fund alone invested directly with voting rights and no lock-up.
DeepSeek is reportedly in early/preliminary talks for a second funding round at a ~$71B pre-transaction valuation, roughly six weeks after the first round closed, with no term sheet signed.

Bloomberg Billionaires Index raised Liang Wenfeng's net worth from ~$16.7B to ~$36B on July 13, 2026, based on an assumed ~78% DeepSeek stake against a ~$50B valuation; the underlying valuation has never been market-tested via a public share trade.

TileLang's canonical repo (tile-ai/tilelang) originated from a Peking University research group with part of the work done during a Microsoft Research internship, open-sourced under MIT license on 2025-01-20; DeepSeek is a downstream adopter, not the creator.

DeepSeek's V4 technical report states TileLang is used to replace hundreds of fine-grained Torch ATen operators with fused kernels, and that TileLang coexists with CUDA kernels rather than fully replacing them.

TileLang added dedicated AscendC and AscendNPU-IR backends targeting Huawei Ascend chips, alongside existing NVIDIA/AMD/Metal/WebGPU backends, confirming it is a kernel-authoring DSL with pluggable codegen rather than a runtime that bypasses vendor toolchains.

TileLang's own paper claims up to 5x speedup over Triton on Nvidia H100 and up to 6x on AMD GPUs, and up to 90% code-size reduction for fused attention kernels — self-reported research-paper benchmarks not independently reproduced by a third party.

DeepSeek-V4's fine-grained Expert-Parallel (MoE) scheme was verified on both Nvidia GPUs and Huawei Ascend NPUs, reporting 1.50-1.73x speedup for general inference and up to 1.96x for long-tail small-batch RL workloads; this is the EP scheduler, not TileLang itself, and absolute Ascend-vs-Nvidia performance was not disclosed.

An earlier 2025 DeepSeek attempt to train on Huawei Ascend chips reportedly failed outright — unstable chips, slow interconnects, immature CANN software, and no FP8 support — forcing a reset back to Nvidia H20s for training, with Ascend relegated to inference duty.

Huawei's Ascend 950PR (unveiled March 20, 2026) is claimed to deliver 1.56 PFLOPS FP4 compute, 112GB in-house HBM (HiBL 1.0), 1.4 TB/s bandwidth, and roughly 2.8x Nvidia H20 performance — figures that trace to Huawei's own disclosure, not an independent teardown.
The 2.8x-2.87x Huawei-vs-H20 performance comparison is methodologically contested because Nvidia's H20 does not natively support the FP4 precision format Huawei used for the comparison.
Reuters reported (citing two anonymous sources) that ByteDance and Alibaba were planning to place Ascend 950PR orders after chip testing 'went well'; no order volume was quantified at that stage.

A thirdhand attribution chain (TrendForce citing Reuters citing The Information) puts combined Alibaba/ByteDance/Tencent Ascend 950 orders at 'hundreds of thousands of units,' with no company confirming an exact figure in an earnings call or IR filing.

SemiAnalysis assessed (pre-950PR, addressing the 910C generation) that Chinese domestic HBM supply from CXMT (~2 million stacks/year), not SMIC logic-die capacity, is the binding constraint on Ascend production volume — sufficient for only ~250,000-300,000 Ascend 910C units.

The newly formed Little Tech Association sent letters on July 22, 2026 to President Trump, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and OSTP Director Michael Kratsios urging against a blanket ban on Chinese open-weight AI models.

The letter's core argument, quoted consistently across outlets: 'American leadership requires two things: world-leading American open-weight models and continued access for U.S. builders to open models already available worldwide.'
'Nearly 200' signatories is a press characterization repeated identically across outlets, not an exact count stated inside the letter itself or a published signatory roster.

The Little Tech Association publicly launched July 13, 2026 as a self-described trade association; its 'Little Tech' branding descends from a16z's July 2024 'Little Tech Agenda' manifesto, though no source confirms direct a16z funding or governance control of the new entity.
Particle founder Suhail Doshi, a signatory, said a ban's chief beneficiary would be Anthropic: 'It's great for Anthropic. We're all going to have to spend money on Anthropic.'

The same day the letter was sent, OSTP Director Kratsios alleged Moonshot AI used a 'sophisticated internal platform' to distill Anthropic's models at scale and acquired export-controlled Nvidia GB300 servers — allegations Moonshot has not publicly addressed.

As of July 20, 2026, no executive order, completed Entity List action, or enacted procurement-ban statute specifically bars U.S. use of Chinese AI models; the administration is reported to be weighing five distinct tools (Entity List threats, procurement rules, security advisories, liability rules, public pressure).

The bipartisan No Adversarial AI Act (H.R. 4142, sponsored by Reps. Moolenaar and Krishnamoorthi) would direct the Federal Acquisition Security Council to maintain a list — updated every 180 days — of adversarial-nation AI models federal agencies cannot purchase or use without exemption; it remains referred to committee, not enacted.

House Homeland Security Chairman Andrew Garbarino and Select Committee on the CCP Chairman John Moolenaar sent formal inquiry letters to Airbnb and Anysphere (Cursor) on April 29, 2026 demanding details on their use of Chinese-built AI systems.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on July 21, 2026: 'We are finding watermarks of our U.S. large language models on many of the Chinese models, and that's unacceptable,' threatening sanctions tied to the Kimi K3 release.

Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 partially launched July 16-17, 2026 (2.8 trillion parameters, full open weights promised July 27, 2026) and is the specific catalyst named across reporting for the administration's renewed restriction push.
White House AI advisor David Sacks publicly accused closed-source labs — 'already a duopoly in terms of model revenue' — of lobbying the government to eliminate their open-source competitors.

BIS added Zhipu AI and 9 subsidiaries to the Entity List effective January 16, 2025, citing advancement of PRC military modernization through AI research; Entity List status legally binds U.S. exporters to the listed entity, not U.S. firms that later use already-downloaded open weights.

Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said Coinbase cut its AI bill nearly in half by defaulting 1,200+ internal AI agents to Entity-Listed Zhipu's GLM-5.2 and Moonshot's Kimi K2.7 Code, downloading the open weights to self-hosted servers.

Huawei's global smartphone shipments fell 41% YoY in Q4 2020 and its global rank dropped from #1 to #6 following the May 2019 Entity List addition (68 non-U.S. affiliates); HiSilicon activated a self-sufficiency 'spare tire' plan the same day, replacing 13,000+ components and redesigning 4,000+ circuit boards by 2023.

ITIF estimates the 2019 Huawei controls cost U.S. firms at least $33B in lost Huawei-related sales (2021-2024), and Huawei's global telecom-equipment market share still stood near 31-34% in 2024, up from ~29% in 2018 — the controls redirected but did not eliminate Huawei's core business.

OpenRouter's own 2025 State of AI report shows Chinese open-source models rising from under 1.2% of weekly token share in late 2024 to nearly 30% in some peak 2025 weeks, averaging ~13% across the full year — though OpenRouter represents roughly 2% of global AI spend by its own account.

Chinese-origin models reportedly processed 12.96 trillion tokens on OpenRouter versus 3.03 trillion for U.S. models (a ~4.3x ratio) in the week of March 30-April 5, 2026 — a figure relayed via Chinese state-affiliated media citing OpenRouter data, not directly verified against OpenRouter's own dashboard.

Snowflake CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy benchmarked GLM-5.2 within 1% of Claude Opus 4.7's performance at roughly one-fifth the per-token cost on an internal 103-task coding suite; Snowflake simultaneously holds a $200M Anthropic partnership.
Lindy CEO Flo Crivello said he moved 100% of the company's model traffic from Anthropic Claude to DeepSeek after inference costs exceeded payroll, projecting millions of dollars in annual savings.

OpenAI's head of strategic futures Dean Ball warned that an open-weight-model-dominant world risks 'full AI communism,' calling it a 'dystopian hellscape,' and separately described a hypothetical tactic of agencies issuing 'soft law' advisories to create regulatory uncertainty around Chinese models.
NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) found DeepSeek models echo Chinese state/CCP narratives roughly four times as often as U.S. reference models on the same evaluation.
Estonia's Foreign Intelligence Service found that DeepSeek 'conceals key information and inserts Chinese propaganda' when answering questions about Estonia's security.

China's Commerce Ministry (MOFCOM) is reportedly drafting export controls that would restrict foreign users' ability to download (though not cloud-access) the weights of Chinese AI models, consulting Alibaba, ByteDance, and Zhipu; separately weighing barring TSMC and Qualcomm from fabricating chips based on Chinese company designs (Huawei, Alibaba, ByteDance).

Days before the MOFCOM report broke, Xi Jinping used the July 2026 World AI Conference in Shanghai to publicly champion open-source AI cooperation and warn against nations prioritizing their own security interests over others' — a rhetorical tension with his own ministry's parallel drafting of controls on China's own open-weight models.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang said on CNBC that Nvidia has 'largely conceded' China's AI-chip market to Huawei, coinciding with Nvidia's China data-center revenue falling to $0 that quarter from $4.6B a year earlier — a collapse driven primarily by U.S. export controls, not demonstrated competitive displacement.

SemiAnalysis's Day-0-to-Day-43 tracking of DeepSeek V4 across Huawei Ascend, Nvidia GB300, AMD MI355X, and Nvidia B200 concluded Huawei 'has proven it can sling a stone on Day 0; whether it can fell a moving giant is yet to be seen' — a durable threat to Nvidia's ecosystem lead remains unproven.

Legal scholarship (Rozenshtein, Lawfare, April 2024) argues there is no general First Amendment right to distribute machine-learning model weights, distinguishing weights (functional, machine-readable) from source code in the Bernstein v. DOJ / Junger v. Daley 'code as speech' lineage — a March 2026 SSRN paper (Trevino & Nikolich) reaches the same conclusion.

Brookings' Kyle Chan argues it is 'ultimately impossible' to ban Chinese open-source AI models once their weights are freely available on the internet — the framing driving Washington's shift toward procurement-only restrictions rather than a possession ban.

No GAO or CRS report, and no named Congressional testimony transcript, was found directly addressing the technical enforcement feasibility of a ban on already-downloaded open-weight models — a genuine evidence gap in the public record as of this research pass.
